data.title: Chipotle: Huge Turnaround Opportunity Vs. Demise Of A Great Brand
data.description:
The last 16 months weren't great for Chipotle (CMG), to say the least. A PR crisis on meal proportions, the leaving of legendary CEO Brian Niccol, and dropping to the bottom of the leaderboard for industry same-store growth have all combined to make Chipotle one of the worst performers in the S&P 500.

Whatever the cause may be, Chipotle is no longer viewed by investors as a best-in-class business, and it's difficult to argue against it in light of recent results.

The question is whether we're witnessing Chipotle's demise or if it's just a tougher period that will eventually pass. If it's the latter, this might be an extraordinary turnaround investment opportunity.

Let's dive in.

Chipotle's "Year From Hell"

When Bill Simmons says a sports team is having a "year from hell," he typically means that it simply can't catch a break, whether it's injuries, harsh calls, a tough schedule, or bad beats; everything is stacking up against it.

Well, it's difficult to deny that Chipotle has been having a "year from hell," or, more precisely, 16 months from hell.

It started with a PR crisis amid viral social media complaints about meal proportions back in mid-2024. A few months later, legendary CEO Brian Niccol decided to leave for Starbucks (SBUX). Then, it's been mainly a very tough macro environment, persistent quality complaints, and market share losses to price promoters.

All in all, Chipotle could really use a break.

Chipotle's Results Have Been Deteriorating

Over the years, investors grew accustomed to seeing mid-teens revenue growth from Chipotle, alongside margin expansion. The consistency of results is what justified paying a high premium for the stock, as investors and analysts expected to see this trajectory continuing for a very long time.

Well, Q3 was another quarter that put a huge dent in investors' confidence. Revenue increased 7.5% to $3.0 billion, but almost all of it was derived from new store openings, and not all growth is treated equally. Comparable sales of 0.3% turned positive, but it was due to a 1.1% increase in average check, offset by a 0.8% decline in traffic.

With comparable sales growing lower than inflation, and with the company trying to fix its proportion problems, margins had to come down. Restaurant-level margins dropped 100 bps to 24.5%, and operating margins dropped a similar 100 bps to 15.9%.

Looking ahead, Chipotle cut its guidance for comparable sales in 2025, now expecting a decline, and maintained its openings outlook for the year. For 2026, they expect to accelerate store openings, a testament to their confidence in being able to revive same-store growth.

All in all, it's hard to underwrite a mid-teens revenue growth & high-teens EPS growth future when results look like this today. It requires a leap of faith and confidence that investors don't seem to have.

Chipotle Is Losing Ground To Competitors

It seems that a budget-conscious consumer is turning to promotions or home cooking, and a higher-income consumer is turning to DoorDash (DASH) and Uber Eats (UBER) for a more diversified lunch rotation. Either way, Chipotle is ranking quite low in the sector.

I do think it's worth noting that Chipotle is dealing with tougher comps than most of its peers, and it could very well be the reason for its much inferior performance. It will be interesting to see how it ranks in future quarters, especially starting Q1-26, when comps get much easier.

Efforts To Turn Things Around Haven't Moved The Needle So Far

Chipotle hasn't been resting on its laurels in wait for things to turn around. It seems that management is determined to do everything in its power to change Chipotle's fortunes.

They increased marketing activity heavily on TV and other platforms. They are also doing many more campaigns than they used to, with colleges, service members, holidays, and rewards members.

They've also been pushing more menu innovation, planning to increase the cadence of limited-time offerings, and launching more extras, including the recently announced Red Chimichurri dip.

What they haven't been doing, and perhaps they will have to eventually, is offering value meals or discounts. The only thing that came close to it is their new group order option, which was released with a $10 coupon, but that's far from the $7 value meals its competitors are offering.

For now, I think it makes sense for management to try and preserve their brand image as an always-on value proposition. Once you start doing these value campaigns, it's difficult to stop.

However, if they continue to bleed share, they might have to relent on that front.

Valuation & History Are The Main Things To Rely On

I think that the idea of a relatively healthy, fresh, tasty, satisfying, and quick meal for $13 remains quite appealing. I believe that this is a category that has seen increased competition, but it's still gaining share overall, and I expect this will continue.

Aside from this qualitative argument that, at some point, Chipotle should regain popularity, investors are left leaning on valuation.

At $34 a share, Chipotle trades at 29 times forward earnings and 28 times next year's estimates, which are quite underwhelming as they bake in just 5% growth in 2026.

This places Chipotle at a valuation level that's not too far above much more mature peers. If and when comparable sales growth accelerates to 2% or higher, Chipotle is likely to return to 15%+ EPS growth and fetch a much higher multiple.

Until it happens, though, I think we can argue that it reached some type of valuation floor, that is, if things don't deteriorate further.

Conclusion

Chipotle has been having a year from hell, and I don't envy its new CEO entering right when things got very tough.

I think that as long as the macro picture doesn't turn worse, things shouldn't deteriorate further for Chipotle, providing a valuation floor.

Meanwhile, I believe Q1-26 is when things will start to look much better, at which point both consensus estimates and the valuation multiple are likely to expand.

As such, I think the current risk/reward is quite attractive, and reiterate Chipotle at a "Buy."

data.title: AXIA Energia: Buy Given Significant +25% Capital Return Expected
data.description:
Almost three years ago I wrote my very first article in Seeking Alpha about Axia Energia (AXIA), which at the time was still called Eletrobras. With a price of $7.69, I slapped a Strong Buy expecting significant pricing gains from the change to Free Market prices from the Quota Regime (i.e. state-controlled prices) in its hydroelectric plants following the privatization of the company.

Since then, Total Return for Axia is around 68% while the S&P500 posted almost 75% - not a bad outcome overall, but a poor one given the Strong Buy rating. Still, as recently as December 2024, investors had the chance to buy this company for $5.60, which would have dramatically improved Axia's return compared to the S&P500.

In this piece I'll revisit some of my assumptions from that first article, identifying what is going according to plan and what isn't. But most importantly I'll show how the Free Cash Flow has been consistent and growing, allowing for significant shareholder return, including the new policy laid out by the company on November 27th, anticipating the start of a dividend tax in 2026. Axia Energia is now a Buy after showcasing that it can flow its pricing gains directly to its Free Cash Flow.

Company Overview and Privatization Recap

Axia Energia was privatized in June of 2022 following a share issuance that raised BRL 34B for the company in order to reimburse the government for all the hydroelectric plants operating under an Operation and Maintenance ((O&M)) regime that the company would keep. The benefit for Axia and its shareholders is that with this reimbursement, Axia was now free to sell the energy generated by the O&M hydroelectric plants in the free market - at the time, the government used to pay BRL 71/MWh, whereas the private market paid BRL 250/MWh.

Since in 2022 Revenue related to the O&M plants was between BRL 4B and BRL 5B, my assumption was that by tripling the prices the Revenue would increase by BRL 10B with no increase in costs or expenses. This was the main enticement for shareholders with Axia Energia privatization and an example can be seen in the image below showing BRL +1B in Contribution Margin YoY coming from the private and short-term market. There were other benefits that I listed at the time, such as: decreased government ownership, which lost its majority and was capped at 10% of voting power; deconsolidation of Itaipu and Eletronuclear by shifting ownership to the government; and liberty to make the company more efficient by incorporating smaller hydroelectric plants not fully owned and promoting early retirement programs or layoffs.

And while the majority of these changes have happened in the past three years, I didn't anticipate three things that weighted in the company's share price. First, less than six months after privatization Lula took office as President of Brazil and dedicated part of his first year to attack Axia's privatization and fight to recover some control of the company. This brought uncertainty to the company until March this year when the parties settled by maintaining the 10% voting power cap but giving the government three more seats in the board.

Second, prices for the new energy sold in the private market were wobbly, at best. Several headwinds occurred since 2022, namely higher rain volume that filled hydroelectric reservoirs (thus lowering energy prices), a spike in solar and wind energy and the sole difficulty of building commercial capability to sell the additional energy capacity. While solar and wind will continue to expand, 2026 is expected to see the consolidation of La Niña bringing dryer conditions to the South and Southeast where important hydroelectric plants are located (thus raising prices) and with three years of experience Axia is now more capable of selling its extra energy in the market.

Third, although I stated in my first article that Eletronuclear - owner of the Angra dos Reis Nuclear Power Plant - would be given back to the government, Axia still remained on the hook for BRL 8B in construction loans, retaining a significant risk since the Angra III, as the reactor under construction is known, has faced significant delays. The company sold its remaining stake in Eletronuclear during Q3'25, booking a significant accounting loss, but decreasing its exposure.

Now, the company has finally left in the past discussions with the government over its privatization, lower energy prices and commercialization capabilities have improved and the benefits of restructurings are seen in its Income Statement.

Financials

So, after three years, how did my forecast of a boost in Revenue and Free Cash Flow held up? Fortunately, pretty well. In the first graph I built the rolling last twelve months of Revenue as reported in each quarter, excluding Revenue from thermal plants since they were sold this year. Between 2023 and 2025, the company increased Revenue by more than BRL 6B. Given the transition from Quota Regime to Free Market prices would be during five years with 20% incremental each year, BRL 6B in the first three years is on track to deliver the BRL 10B I mentioned earlier.

A quick peak under the hood shows that this increment is coming from the right place. O&M Revenue decreased from BRL 3.4B to BRL 2.0B, while Revenue from Renewable Generation and Short-Term Energy grew from BRL 12.3B to BRL 18.7B. It's tough to separate exactly what comes from higher energy volume sold, what's price and what's new wind or solar plants, but the direction points to it being mainly from the shift from Quota to Free Market prices. Transmission is also growing at a healthy pace and should continue to boost grow in the future, specially once the shift from Quota to Free Market prices is completed.

Another important topic is the efficiency that management promised to achieve once the company was privatized. If we sum costs and expenses, the growth has been very muted. In three years the company has grown its costs and expenses around 5% (in total, not yearly). With costs and expenses flat, we can expect all of that Revenue increase to flow directly to the bottom-line.

However, instead of showing you the last twelve months of Net Income, I prefer to show you the Free Cash Flow statement. There are two reasons for this: the first is that the company has a complex balance sheet and with litigation provisions, businesses divestitures and other hits to its Income Statement, the Net Income might not paint the best picture to fully analyze the company. Instead, look at the performance of Cash Flow from Operations (CFO), Capital Expenditures and Free Cash Flow (FCF), also on a twelve months rolling basis.

Not only has the company grown its CFO by more than BRL 8B in three years, but it has also continued to invest, mainly in Transmission lines. FCF has jumped from around BRL 2B to close to BRL 9B. There are a few other things we should consider in the Valuation section afterwards: the continued boost from Quota to Market Prices, the potential to decrease Interest Expense and the expected Debt to Equity Ratio. But before we deep dive on Valuation, I want to remember readers that the company will pay BRL 8B in Dividends this year (which, judging by the numbers above, it can comfortably continue to do so in the coming years), but it has also announced a new strategy to increase shareholder return in the coming years.

New +25% Shareholder Return Strategy

On November 27th the company announced to the market its strategy to increase shareholder return while also protecting investors from the new dividend tax to be introduced in Brazil in 2026. If you are not familiar with this new withholding tax in Brazil, suffice to say that foreign investors will now be taxed at 10% for all dividends paid by a Brazilian company. Brazilian investors will also be taxed, but only if they earn more than BRL 50,000 (almost $10k USD) per month from the same legal entity.

Several companies are already anticipating dividends that would have been paid in 2026 to 2025, but the case of Axia Energia is a little more complex. It does have a lot of cash in its balance sheet - close to BRL 30B as of Q3'25 or ~19% of its market cap - but it also has BRL 73B in Debt, BRL 20B in Legal Provisions and BRL 39B in Obligations to a sectoral fund that invests in public policy related to energy. This last one was assumed as part of its privatization process.

To guarantee that it will continue to pay dividends in the coming years but also maintain flexibility to pay down debt, settle its legal cases and amortize the obligations, the company decided for a different to strategy to signal the market its willingness towards high shareholder return: the issuance of a new class of preferential shares to existing shareholders and a promise to either convert these shares to ordinary shares or retire them through buyback. The company expects to incorporate close to BRL 40B into its Equity to distribute to shareholders as this new preferential share.

Without this new scheme, if the company paid BRL 40B in dividends during the coming years, investors would receive BRL 36B - decreasing the shareholder return from +25% to +22%. Now, the company will award each shareholder of ordinary shares ~25% of its current number of shares as a new preferential share that will be either redeemed by the company or converted to an ordinary share until 2031. Since this new share usually has a cost amount associated to it (expect it to be announced alongside the proposal approval on December 19th), it should work as a tax shield as investors will pay a tax only on their capital gain.

Since we are talking about issuance of 25% of current number of shares, the company share price should dip by 25% once this process is finalized in order to keep the market cap the same. However, one other option is that even with the new issuance the share price might hold, allowing the market cap to increase. A similar situation occurred when the company was privatized: the company was worth BRL 60B and the issuance of new shares made the company worth BRL 100B since the share price held steady. This outcome will be known soon, but the bottom line is that current shareholders are receiving a guaranteed ~5% return per year until 2031 with significant tax incentives, plus regular dividends (already confirmed 5% distribution in 2025) and price appreciation as the company is still undervalued.

Valuation

Finally, let's wrap up with the Valuation of Axia Energia. As shown, the company currently generates close to BRL 9B in FCF, with potential BRL 4~5B more given the continued shift from Quota to Free Market prices. Axia also pays almost BRL 10B in Interest Expense for a current BRL 70B in outstanding Debt. This implies almost 15% of Interest Rate on Debt, which is in line with current Interest Rate practiced by Brazil's Central Bank. Different than other countries, Brazil has yet to start its process of monetary easing, having held Interest Rate at 15% since June 2025. It's largely expected that rates will start to go down in early 2026, hopefully landing in 10% by late 2027.

If in two years the Interest Expense is cut by one third, then the company could potentially earn more than BRL 3B from a decrease in this line. To be fair, it would also decrease its Interest Income, if it continues to hold the current amount of cash, by BRL 1.5B. In the end, Axia Energia could boost its FCF by BRL 1.5B with a decrease in Interest Rates. Sure, the company could continue to raise Debt, but its current capital structure is conservative and I'd expect them to only raise Debt if they have an interesting Transmission opportunity in their hands. If they repurchase all the shares for BRL 40B the ratio below would go to 1.

So considering the current BRL 9B of FCF, plus BRL 4B from Quota to Free Market prices and BRL 1.5B from lower Interest Expense, we have a very comfortable path towards BRL 15B per year between 2027 and 2028. This is more than enough to cover the current BRL 8B dividends (which translates to a 5% Yield) and the BRL 40B buyback in the next five years (even without using the current Cash balance). There is also significant opportunities in Transmission that the company now has the cash and the willingness to pursue - but, since this is not a certainty, I'll keep out of the Valuation and should consider a bonus or margin of safety.

Considering that I don't believe the company needs to pay down debt, it can funnel the entire BRL 15B towards shareholder return. Below you find the Discounted Cash Flow Model which shows possible upsides of 126% and 50% depending on the Interest Rate and the Discount Rate in the near future. However, it's important to remember that if the company doubles its price in five years, the investor will receive not 100% but 150% including potential Dividends and Buybacks.

Translating this valuation to USD, the scenario with 8% Discount Rate has a Market Cap of $53B and 10% Discount Rate has $42B (using an FX Rate of 5.40). Although not a Strong Buy anymore, this company is a solid Buy, benefitting from significant structural changes in its pricing power, a decrease in Interest Rates in Brazil and a possible spike in energy prices due to La Niña in 2026.

Risks

Government risk should always be first and foremost in a developing economy in Latin America. So even if Axia Energia has reached a settlement with the government, it's impossible to guarantee with certainty that after next year elections there won't be any new demand or challenges towards Axia Energia, specially if energy prices start to rise. Still, Axia Energia privatization happened on solid legal foundation, as we have seen in the past three years.

One important risk that will continue to exist is exchange risk. The dollar depreciated during 2025 and the Brazilian Real was one of the main beneficiaries, gaining almost 20% against the USD. That tide could turn in 2026, in part because there will be national elections in October. So investors may face a rollercoaster at first if the BRL depreciation in 2026 is stronger than the stock price performance. Finally, another risk that is important to raise is any challenge the government might make towards future expansion in Transmission lines. Axia is a significant player and if the government judges it to be too big in the market, it may curtail its capacity to expand.

Final Remarks

Axia Energia is a very profitable and diversified company with strong tailwinds identified. But even with a company like this, timing is very important. Investors who followed my advice saw two years of stagnating returns until 2025 when the market really woke up to its capacity to generate predictable cash flows. 2026 is an election year and is hard to imagine what will occur during the months until October in Brazil, but it could be a roller coaster. The overall Brazilian market could also wobble a little after a great run this year, but the fundamentals of Axia Energia will be intact and Free Cash Flow should continue to grow, thus making this company a Buy.

data.title: Himax Technologies: Hold Until Profitability Stabilizes And Emerging Technologies Contribute Materially
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Himax Technologies (HIMX) is attempting to pivot away from the commoditized Display Driver Integrated Circuit (DDIC) towards a business that enables both Automotive Touch and Display Driver Integration (TDDI) and AI sensing (WiseEye). The shift would be net positive for the company as it would increase the average selling price, boost margins while moving up the company in the value chain. In my opinion, once the shift is complete, this would allow a decent re-rating in multiples.

Valuation-wise, Himax trades at a 30-50% discount vs the semiconductor sector. However, I believe it is justified as it is highly dependent on the automotive industry, sales in China and its fabless business model focused on commodity Display Driver ICs (DDICs). Himax trades at a +10-25% premium to Silicon Motion Technology (SIMO), its closest peer, which I find surprising and could signal overvaluation given the fact that SIMO has a more stable growth and a stronger financial profile.

My recommendation is to hold until we see stabilization in falling revenues and profitability. The shift towards “Intelligent Displays” in automotive cockpit technology is clear, but still needs to be seen in quarterly numbers. Given that the management guided us towards a flat Q4, I believe this is not the time to be invested in Himax just yet.

What does the company actually do?

Himax is a fabless semiconductor company focusing on display imaging processing. It means that it provides integrated circuits (ICs) for a wide range of devices such as TVs, mobile phones, PCs, and automobiles. Operationally, it has two segments: Driver ICs (DDICs) and non driver products.

The core business of the company is to sell Display Driver ICs (DDICs) to customers such as Lenovo, LG Display, Panasonic, Samsung, Vivo, Amazon, etc. These integrated circuits (ICs) serve as an interface between a device's main processor (CPU/GPU) and the display panel itself (LCD, OLED or LED). It is sort of a “brain” that translates digital data from the processor into electrical signals that will illuminate pixels on the screen. DDICs besides their function of signal transmission and pixel control, are also regulating power supplied to the display.

The IC business tends to be commoditized; this is why Himax competes to position itself as a provider of “Intelligent Display” in automotive cockpit technology. This is where the Touch and Display Driver Integration (TDDI) technology is the first step to build a durable competitive edge. Besides this new tech, to differentiate itself, Himax combines both Display Driver IC (DDIC) and the Touch Controller IC (OTCQB:TCON) into one semiconductor, simplifying the supply chain and assembly for the automakers.

The second step for Himax to carve out its market share is to build an Ultralow Power AI Sensing platform that integrates an always-on CMOS image sensor, an AI processor (NPU/AI engine), and a CNN-based AI algorithm. This new sort of WiseEye sensing technology adds additional contextual awareness to the entire cockpit. The role of the AI sensing is to actively eye-track and gaze-analyze any sign of conductor distraction, inattention by tracking pupil movements and blinking. Other features would include driver face recognition or specific hand gestures for convenience and driver safety.

Last but not least, Himax is positioning itself as a contender in the augmented reality (AR) and optical solutions in spatial computing. These products are high added value with high-growth potential. A summary of the Himax innovations is included below for reference from the Q3 2025 report.

Himax Technologies today is a $1.3 billion market cap company with a $1.6 billion enterprise value, which implies a 1.9x Fwd EV/ Revenue, 17.0x EBITDA and 26.0x PE multiples. Estimated 2025 leverage is at 3.8x. More detailed information about the bridge is presented below.

Digitalization of the vehicle cockpit and adoption of the next-generation display technologies are the main growth drivers for Himax and in that respect, the company is well positioned as it holds a 40% lobal market share in Driver IC for automotive displays.

The Share Price Evolution Reflects Investors Concerns

The price chart below comparing Himax and Fabless Semi ETF performance reveals short-term volatility and cyclical pressure that impact Himax from Q2 2025 onwards. A decoupling in performance from that point in time is likely to be related to Himax's high inventories and soft demand in the auto-industry. Since Q3 2025, Himax has failed to recover its prior highs in January 2025.

Today, Himax's share price stays below its anchored WVAP that is likely to act as resistance, which means sellers are in control. Such a trend will probably persist as long as investors are still concerned with weak demand for legacy DDIC products and declining China sales.

Most Recent Quarterly Performance

Looking at the largest Q3 2025 report numbers, we can see that there is a disconnect between short-term financial pressure in Q3 2025, an eventual stabilization in Q4, and the long-term growth potential of Himax.

As presented in the slide above, Q3 2025 revenues decreased 10.5% YoY and 7.3% sequentially. Reasons invoked by the management during the earnings call are a 23.6% decrease in Large Display segment and a weaker demand in end markets for TVs and monitors. These combined factors have led to an operating quarterly loss of $0.6 million, a negative margin of 0.3% compared to a positive +8.4% operating margin from the previous quarter.

Earnings decreased 92% to $0.01 per share. Such underperformance can be explained by inventory correction as well as a large, non-recurring employee annual bonus expense of $ 8.1 million ($13.9 million in Q3 2024). Soft Q3 2025 revenues make the bonus impact particularly severe on earnings.

Financial Projections

Himax units sold and revenues have been on the decline since 2021, as we can see on the slide below. Sales in the rest of the world are more or less stable, while sales from China are decreasing rapidly. The company, however, is able to preserve the gross margins in the 40-30% range.

Reading the 3Q25 presentation, the management guides us toward a flat Q4 2025 revenue and gross margins. Then I assume $199 million in revenues for Q4, which allows me to sum up all the quarterly revenues and compute a FY 2025 revenue of 828 million, an 8.7% decline from 2024. I assume FY 2025 EBITDA and EBIT margins at 10.3% and 6.0%, equal to the realized margins as per 9 months of 2025. I expect FY2025 net income to be around $50-52m range and the cash flow to be negative. For FY 2026 and 2027 revenue growth, I rely on the analyst consensus of +13.42% and +10.27% sequentially. I believe such growth is justified given the pivot beyond traditional driver IC business. A progressive ramp-up towards non-driver technologies like Wise-Eye AI and Co-Packaged Optics (CPO) in 2026-2027 seems reasonable, as these technologies are currently in a test phase and will only enter mass production by 2026. The shift towards higher value products, as well as inventory normalization, will allow some margin expansion. I assume in the table below both EBITDA and EBIT margin expansions of +100pbs per year.

Himax is 10-20% overvalued compared to its closest peer, Silicon Motion Technology

For the valuation exercise, I compute trading multiples and compare them to sector median and the closest peer in the semiconductor industry, Silicon Motion Technology. Both companies have a core business in ICs, are fabless with exposure to cyclical trends of memory and display markets. Revenues are highly dependent on smartphones, tablets and automotive displays. Both companies are shifting towards higher value segments, but the main difference is that Himax focuses on Automotive TDDI and AI sensing (WiseEye) whereas Silicon Motion is focusing more on Enterprise SSD controllers and high-end client controllers. In the end, Silicon Motion is more exposed to PC/Data center demand. Enterprise SSD controllers are usually more stable and a mission critical segment, therefore grant a premium in the valuation multiple.

The table below presents FY 2025e and FY 2026e EV/Sales, EV/ EBITDA and P/E multiples. Data indicates that Himax is trading at a large discount compared to its semiconductor peers median. For example, on the FY 2025e basis, Himax trades at a 31% EV/EBITDA discount vs. median sector and 29% discount to Silicon Motion. When looking at FY 2026, the discount remains at 51% vs. the sector median, but no longer persist vs. closest peer. Himax FY2026 EV/EBITDA multiple is 11% higher than Silicon Motion’s. P/E is 24% higher. This is surprising, as Silicon is growing faster and has higher margins and virtually no debt. Plus, we discussed the strategic positioning in the NAND storage ecosystem of Silicon Motion that should command a higher multiple.

Therefore, I conclude that based on EV/EBITDA and P/E multiples, Himax is overvalued vs. its closest peer by 10-25%. Given the recent softness in the quarterly results, I remain cautious on the valuation and would like to see improved guidance for 2026 and 2027 before jumping in.

A catalyst for a successful margin expansion would be a successful transition in Automotive TDDI/TCON and WiseEye AI/CPO. If the projections above are too conservative, growth could accelerate, multiples would come down, and make Himax attractive for value investors.

For completeness, I include below the backup table with semiconductor trading peers’ multiples. These are the numbers used in the Himax valuation comparison table. The benchmark is composed Semiconductor players that I think provide good data points. That being said, I believe Silicon Motion Technology stays the best compared to Himax for the reasons we already discussed above, and as multiple dispersion in the semiconductor industry is quite wide.

Important Risks Involved

By nature, Himax operates in a cyclical industry, as it is dependent on its end-markets mentioned previously. Therefore, revenues have generally low visibility given the macro uncertainties.

DDIC market is highly competitive, which limits Himax's pricing power. In the Q3 2025 report, the company mentions it holds a 7% market share. Market leaders are Samsung (26%) and Novatek (22%). These companies are much bigger and have potentially lower manufacturing costs that gives them the ability to push selling prices lower. LX Semicon (10%) and Raydium (7%) add competitive pressure and in a high-volume business such as this one. Therefore, Himax’s profit margins could be at risk because of its #5 position.

Failure to innovate would put the company’s future at risk. Indeed, much of the future growth is dependent on emerging technologies that are still in the validation phase, with mass production starting only in 2026. Solutions such as WiseEye sensing, co packaged optics, and smart glasses/AR/VR-related applications still have a limited impact on the current sales. My estimate for Q3 2025 is that this part of the business will represent 7.7% of the Q3 2025 revenue. The non driver segment is 19.7% of the total Q3 2025, where Tcon business is 12% of the total.

Estimated net debt/EBITDA is around 3.8x in 2025, which is significant for a fabless semiconductor company. However, the company has a history of having a strong position of cash ($ 257 million in Q3 2025) and covering its finance costs with interest income (+$4.9 million net interest income for 9 months of 2025)

Other risks include trade tensions with China as a Taiwanese company and FX risk.

Investment Decision

This is a case of a company where the core business competes on volume in the DDIC market dominated by Samsung and Novatek. Himax is forced to innovate to survive, and it is well positioned to capture future growth from emerging segments such as Automotive Touch and Display Driver Integration (TDDI) and AI sensing (WiseEye).

Financially, units sold and revenues have been on the structural decline since 2021, partly due to soft demand in auto and sales in China. Q3 2025 was extremely negative, and the management guided only to a “flat Q4 2025”. I remain cautious and would like to see numbers stabilize. Today President D. Trump proposed a sharp rollback in U.S. vehicle fuel economy rules, that should stimulate auto demand, but I believe it will take time before impacting Himax revenues.

Valuation wise, Himax is overvalued compared to its closest peer by 10-25%. There is a long list of risks mentioned above that, in my opinion, consequential. I would like to see emerging technologies push revenues sharply higher before investing in Himax.

data.title: Silvercorp Metals: Building A Better Business, But Not Yet A Better Valuation
data.description:
Introduction

Silvercorp Metals (SVM) has used the cash flow from its China silver mining operation to jumpstart a multijurisdictional copper-gold business. That growth potential should factor into a valuation. However, it is prudent to value mining based on operational mines, not on prospects, since there are many hurdles to cross before a mine becomes profitable. As we will soon see, what initially looked like a valuation mismatch based on the current stage versus prospects disappears when the numbers are examined closely.

Q2 Operating Performance

As we see from their latest Q2 2026 earnings, the entire free cash flow for Silvercorp is now generated in China. Revenue was $83.3 million, up 23% from the previous year. This growth came from both higher pricing for silver and gold, as well as from a higher volume of gold selling. Operating cash flow reached $39.2 million, up 69%. Adjusted net income came in at $22.6 million, or $0.10 per share, which, given SVM’s single-digit share price, is a meaningful profitability level.

If you only skim filings, you may misinterpret the net loss of $11.5 million. Looking deeper, we can see that the loss was not operational but rather due to a one-time fair-value adjustment to derivative liabilities totaling $53 million and did not involve cash leaving the business. I can see that the quarter was actually profitable once I remove the non-cash and one-time items. SVM also generated a positive FCF of $11.4 million. The balance sheet remained strong, closing with approximately $382 million in cash and another $180 million in investments.

China Operations

The economic core remains in China, specifically the Ying Mining District and the GC Mine. Here, consolidated production reached 1.7 million ounces of silver, 2,085 ounces of gold, 14 million pounds of lead and 5.6 million pounds of zinc in Q2. Silver production remained flat, but lead and especially gold surged, the latter rising 76% yoy. Usually, gold and silver do not move in tandem. At least, they do not move at the same pace. So SVM’s focus on gold stabilizes revenue volatility. The company was thus able to produce $40.8 million in mining operating income in Q2. Ying alone contributed over 93% of that number. However, there has been a rise in AISC (all-in sustaining costs) per silver ounce, from $13.94 compared to $11.66 the prior year, a 20% rise. This is going to persist.

Despite the higher AISC and royalties and so on, free cash flow has remained positive. This cash flow that it is generating in China is being used not to pay dividends, or to fund marginal expansions, or even buybacks. The company has undertaken a massive project of channeling this cash flow into major growth projects halfway around the world from China.

The El Domo Development

This future growth centers on the El Domo copper-gold project in Ecuador. This project is being built without dilution, which is atypical in an industry where dilutive funding in early stages often leaves early shareholders in the lurch. SVM has flipped that model, using the Chinese cash as well as the previously secured Wheaton streaming facility to fund the Ecuador build-out.

El Domo mine has seen strong progress. They faced some early weather-related disruptions, but the pace has now picked up strongly, and they also placed equipment orders for $22.9 million.

As a result, SVM drew the first tranche of $43.9 million from their $175.5 million stream facility at the end of Q2. This facility is with Wheaton Precious Metals, carried forward from Adventus, the previous owner of El Domo. Management decided not to replace/renegotiate the agreement because it was structured when gold prices were lower. Higher prices remove any rationale to change the deal.

The company now has to execute the buildout. There were weather disruptions early on, given a wetter-than-normal Ecuadorian season. Pace has, however, picked up now. Other risks like productivity during ramp-up, strip ratio sequencing, supply chain execution and power are normal operational risks with new mines. So while El Domo continues, the company has hedged its bets with a 28 percent equity interest in New Pacific Metals, which provides indirect exposure to two open-pit silver projects in Bolivia, Silver Sand and Carangas.

There is no operating or capital commitment required, so this ownership basically behaves like a call option on the success of someone else’s development. While this is not directly relevant to the investment case for SVM, it remains an optionality.

A Sum of Parts (SoTP) valuation

In order to value SVM today, the growth prospects must be taken into account. A basic discounted cash flow model does not work given the uncertainty of El Domo’s timeline and rates. We must use a sum-of-the-parts valuation (SoTP), with conservative assumptions applied at each layer. These layers are basically five: the current cash flow at the China mines; El Domo as a risk-adjusted near-term cash-flow asset; Kuanping as a smaller satellite expansion within Ying; the equity portfolio; and net cash.

The China mines, with their current cash generation and demonstrated mine life, are the easiest to value. I get a $725 million valuation for Ying and GC combined, using annualized fiscal 2026 operating cash flow, minus non-recurring cash offsets of around $145 million, and a conservative 5x cash flow multiple. This is not aggressive in any way, since high-grade long-life underground producers often command 6–8x in bullish times, and 4–6x in neutral ones. The China operations merit the center of the range under current metals pricing.

DCF is theoretically ideal, but in practice it is misleading when it comes to El Domo. That is because of imprecision in early-stage ramp assumptions. It is cleaner to use a risk-adjusted NAV assumption tied to consensus valuation. Per the investor presentation, consensus NAV estimate is around $2.0 billion for the whole business versus a ~$1.59 billion market cap. To isolate El Domo, recall that most analysts tracking Adventus before the acquisition commonly valued El Domo based on the 2021 Feasibility study. This study reported an after-tax NPV (8%) of US$259 million at base-case metal prices and a 32% after-tax IRR. Discounting 45% for construction and execution risks, I get a fair value of $142 million for El Domo. That number is reasonable for a project under construction with financing in place but commissioning still ahead.

I don’t consider Kuanping large enough to contribute to valuation by itself. Kuanping does help increase permitted throughput in Ying to 1.52 million tonnes per year. I can reasonably assign $50–$70 million to Kuanping, since it augments the China production. However, it should not be valued as a separate asset. I have taken $60 million as the midpoint.

Moving on to the equity portfolio, $180 million is what SVM reported in investments. I am taking this at market value, no appreciation factored in. Net cash is straightforward: $382 million in cash minus $115 million in debt produces $267 million.

Summing these parts yields a conservative SoTP of approximately:

China operations (cash-flow multiple) – $725MEl Domo (risk-adjusted NAV) – $142MKuanping – $60MEquity portfolio – $180MNet cash – $267M

Total around $1.374 billion

The market cap is $1.59 billion, so the SoTP valuation is ~13.6% below market cap. This SoTP valuation cannot argue for SVM being materially undervalued today. The current valuation gap is therefore not the basis of a Buy case.

However, my model is conservative on metals pricing and cuts the numbers by a steep 45% discount to El Domo’s demonstrated after-tax NPV from the 2021 Feasibility Study. I have also not included any upside from throughput growth in China, and I have kept Kuanping’s contribution modest. In other words, my model is valuing Silvercorp as it is today, not what it may become once El Domo reaches production.

So the analytical basis is not precision, but asymmetry. My model captures the downside against the current market valuation - and it is close. The upside, however, is not captured at all.

So if El Domo goes through construction without major delays and starts contributing cash flow at anything near feasibility levels, my model needs to adjust for that. Reducing the risk discount from 45% to a more typical 30% for a funded construction-stage project would lift SoTP a little higher, but it would still not go above market cap. Even if I remove all discounts and assign El Domo its full Feasibility study NPV ($259 million), I get a SoTP of $1.49 billion - still below market cap. In other words, nothing in my math supports a buy today.

Risks to the Neutral Thesis

My neutral thesis is based on the conclusion that the current market valuation is close to the value of the China operations plus a partial valuation of El Domo. So there is not enough margin of safety for a buy thesis. The key risk - to my neutral thesis - is if fundamentals improve faster than what I have assumed.

The first, and key, risk to my neutral stance is if SVM executes better than expected. If China operating cash flow rises 15-20%, pushing China valuation to $850 million, and El Domo is totally de-risked and valued closer to future cash flow at around $300 million, then here’s how the SoTP will look:

  • China: 850M

  • El Domo: 300M

  • Kuanping: 60M

  • Equity portfolio: 180M

  • Net cash: 267M

Total = 850 + 300 + 60 + 180 + 267 = 1,657 → $1.657B.

That is around 4% above current market cap - still not huge, but at least in this model, SVM is being valued as a two-engine cash flow machine.

The next risk is typical of the equity market, a sentiment mismatch. So if the market rewards progress even before cash flow is visible, repricing El Domo based on construction milestones, the valuation could improve. In such a scenario, waiting for physical cash flow to play catch up with valuation might mean buying at a higher price later.

The third risk is that my model assumes base-case metals pricing. If things improve, that could raise SoTP without any operational changes. Obviously, what cannot be predicted cannot be priced in, but there does remain the risk that if copper goes up, that could justify a Buy before commissioning.

Finally, my model assumes that elevated AISC persists. If 2026-27 sees more efficient mechanization at Ying, lifting grade without requiring higher tonnage, margins could recover. That would definitely call for a valuation upgrade even if the El Domo situation remains unchanged.

Conclusion – Neutral

Silvercorp is going through an interesting transition phase; however, as we just saw, the share price already reflects most of that transition. It is rare - and valuable - to see a mining company funding growth from existing cash flow and some debt, but no dilution. So that is good. However, when the multi-scenario SoTP is compared to the current $1.59 billion market cap, there is no real margin to justify a Buy thesis.

So while Silvercorp may be building a better business, there are simply too many factors - many unknown ones - to justify a higher valuation. If China margins improve, if the market rerates based on development alone, a Buy may be justified. Until then, though, the stock correctly reflects the company as it is today, and there is not enough margin of safety to take a forward execution risk. Therefore, I remain Neutral.

data.title: MXF: Stellar 2025, But Possible Slowdown In 2026
data.description:
Introduction

The price of the Mexico Fund, Inc. (MXF), a U.S. based closed-end fund concentrated in Mexcian equities, has increased over 45% since January 2025, stabilizing around the $18-$20 range since the end of September. Investors are tasked to determine whether the price will continue to rise through 2026, continuing towards new highs, or pull back based on weaker economic conditions ahead. My investment thesis is the following: While the current discount to NAV of 14.76% provides a potential valuation buffer, this is in line with historical discount to NAV figures. With modest economic growth expectations, moderate levels of inflation, and a slightly depreciated peso (relative to earlier in 2025), I expect to see difficulty maintaining momentum to the upside, with a possible reset before moving higher. For this reason, I rate MXF a ‘Hold’.

Main Considerations

When determining my ‘Hold’ rating for the Mexico Fund, I consider several factors such as valuation, fund history, economic growth, inflation, and currency stability. While the current discount to NAV of 14.76% may appear attractive for some investors, it is not significantly different from the 52-week average of 17.59%.

On the economic front, GDP growth in Mexico is expected to be moderate in 2026, with expectations ranging between 0.3%-1.9%. While Banco de México provides relatively conservative estimates, the IMF has a more optimistic view of GDP growth in 2026.

Along with moderately positive GDP growth, inflation has been trending down since September 2022 and is expected to reach approximately 3% by the end of 2026. With inflation converging towards lower levels, the peso remains relatively stable. It is currently trading within the range of 18.30 to 18.35 per USD. While this is lower than highs seen earlier in the year, it remains well within the range that it has maintained over the last five years.

Lastly, the Mexico Fund has an inception date of 1981, providing us with over 40 years of performance and management history that we can evaluate. Such a long track record shows how its managers have successfully navigated varying economic environments over decades.

Fund Strategy

The Mexico Fund, Inc. is a US-listed closed-end fund with current assets of $328.25 million. According to the fund’s website, its primary investment objective is long-term capital appreciation, investing in Mexican securities (mainly equities). According to the fund’s holdings information, as of September 30, 2025, holdings include Mexican juggernauts such as Grupo México, Fomento Económico Mexicano (FMX), and Wal-Mart De México S.A.B. de C.V. (OTCQX:WMMVY). MXF’s top ten holdings are included in the chart below.

Top Ten Holdings (65.81% of Net Assets) As of September 30, 2025

1 Grupo México

12.27%

2 Grupo Financiero Banorte

11.70%

3 Fomento Económico Mexicano

10.85%

4 Wal-Mart de México

8.31%

5 América Móvil, S.A.B. de C.V (AMX)

4.80%

6 CEMEX S.A.B. de C.V (CX)

4.65%

7 Gruma S.A.B. de C.V (GMMKY)

3.41%

8 Arca Continental

3.31%

9 Grupo Aeroportuario del Sureste S.A.B. de C.V (ASR)

3.26%

10 Grupo Aeroportuario del Pacífico S.A.B. de C.V (PAC)

3.25%

MXF’s holdings are not overly concentrated in any one sector, with only beverages and financial groups making up greater than 10% of NAV (13.60% and 11.19% respectively). Additional holdings consist of airports, auto parts, building materials, chemical products, consumer products, food, mining, railroads, real estate, and restaurants. Due to the funds’ diversification consisting of many sectors of the Mexican economy, and the modest GDP expectations for 2026, I feel comfortable maintaining my ‘Hold’ position.

Additionally, when we take a more concentrated look at the fund’s major holdings, analysts maintain mixed projections. For example, Grupo México (OTCPK:GMBXF), Grupo Financiero Banorte (OTCQX:GBOOY), and Fomento Económico Mexicano (FMX) maintain analyst ratings ranging from ‘Hold’ to ‘Strong Buy’.

When looking at recent top performers like Cemex, Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings currently show it as a ‘Strong Buy’, with positive valuation, growth, and profitability metrics. However, Cemex has remained highly cyclical over the last 10 years. With the stock currently trading at all-time highs, up over 90% YTD, I believe that it is not unforeseeable that changing economic conditions could lead to a temporary pullback. With Cemex making up nearly 5% of the fund’s holdings, such a pullback could hold back future returns.

Valuation

Since MXF operates as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a discount or premium to the actual value of the underlying assets. Earlier this year, the fund’s discount to NAV reached 23.18%, having since recovered to 14.76%. Today’s discount is in line with the 52-week average discount to NAV of 17.59%.

While the current discount does provide a modest undervaluation compared to the value of the underlying assets, taken into consideration with the historical discount, it is my opinion that this undervaluation does not provide a compelling enough reason for opening/ adding to a position.

Peer Comparison

MXF has outperformed its peers during the last year, returning 44.90%. By comparison, the Mexico Equity & Income Fund (MXE) performed similarly, returning 40.83%, albeit with a higher expense ratio (1.51% compared to MXF’s 1.03%). Additionally, MXF significantly outperformed other peers in its peer profile. including Taiwan Fund, Inc. (TWN), Hartford Capital Appreciation Fund (HHCAX), Fidelity Advisor Healthcare Fund (HHCCX), and NYLI MacKay DefinedTerm Municipal Opportunities Fund (MMD). It's important to note the rather obvious differences in their focus, however, as their holdings reflect different holdings.

In terms of expense ratios, MXF falls somewhat in the middle when compared to its peers. However, when looking at other alternatives to gain Mexican exposure in one’s portfolio, there are seemingly cheaper options. For example, iShares MSCI Mexico ETF (EWW) offers an expense ratio of 0.50%, and has also slightly outperformed MXF on both a YTD and 12-month time frame.

Economic Considerations

Based on the most recent outlook from the Banco de México, the central bank foresees real GDP growth in 2026 of approximately 1.1% (with an expected range between 0.3% to 1.9%). In contrast, The International Monetary Fund has a slightly more optimistic base case scenario, estimating growth at 1.5% for 2026. While growth is expected to continue at a conservative rate, it does present an opportunity cost problem, where perhaps greater returns could be achieved in regions experiencing higher rates of growth.

For inflation, data from October 2025 shows inflation currently at 3.57%, representing a 19 basis point decrease from the prior month. While inflation continues to move in the right direction, with the central bank seeing a continued move towards 3% by mid/end of 2026, some analysts warn that inflation could still remain a persistent issue throughout the next year. For example, inflation in areas such as food, agriculture, and energy continues to remain uncertain, with these sectors being exposed to pressures from global commodity prices.

In the context of Mexico’s economic outlook, the peso remains stable, albeit down from levels seen earlier in the year. Currently, the peso is trading within a range of 18.30 to 18.35. Over the last 12 months this range has been between approximately 18.20 to 21.30 per USD, with the current exchange rate showing a pullback nearer to the bottom of the 12-month range.

Risks/Challenges

Similar to many emerging market funds, MXF is exposed to currency and exchange rate risk coupled with economic risk. Since the underlying holdings of the fund are in pesos, any economic fluctuation could have a negative result on the fund’s performance. Additionally, as a closed-end fund, it is typical for MXF to trade below NAV. While a discount to NAV can often be seen as an undervaluation and a mismatch of purchase price to underlying assets, this discount could widen in adverse market conditions.

A widening of the NAV discount or a depreciation of the peso could ultimately hamper underlying NAV growth. Additionally, as the Mexican economy is correlated to the US, rate volatility could also cause drawdowns. Persistent volatility or regulatory changes could pressure dividends, which would have a negative effect on the appeal of the fund, that being the yield + discount. All in all, given the fund’s performance over the last year, even if the Mexican economy does moderately well throughout the next year, that is no guarantee that the fund will continue to outperform. Political, regulatory, and currency risk will always remain a factor.

According to the company’s most recent semi-annual report, the fund also identifies current global conflicts such as the Israel-Hammas conflict and special military operation in Ukraine as potential risks, resulting in increased volatility to global financial markets and commodity prices. The fund also identifies the uncertain US tariff policy as a potential future risk.

Finally, it is worth noting that MXF remains between the overbought and oversold zone according to the RSI indicator. The current RSI is 59, reflecting that despite the over 40% YTD returns, investors remain somewhat neutral. It is my view that, despite the varying economic forecasts for 2026, current fund holdings consist of well performing businesses that could perform well in an economy with muted growth expectations. This view is supported by current investor sentiment.

Take Away

MXF offers investors a way to gain Central American/Latin American exposure in their portfolios. During the past year, the fund has significantly outperformed the broad market and its peers. However, in light of stellar performance in 2025, and based on conservative economic estimates, and historical discount to NAV, I rate CEE a ‘Hold’. Investors should continue to monitor the economic conditions in the region, the peso exchange rate, and investment allocations of the fund. Additionally, given the fund’s expense ratio of 1.03%, other options with lower expense ratios should also be considered when allocating for Mexican exposure in one’s portfolio.

data.title: Boardwalk REIT: Canadian Residential Real Estate At A 35% Discount
data.description:
Introduction

Some of the Canadian residential REITs have gotten increasingly attractive in the past few months. Boardwalk REIT (BEI.UN:CA) (OTCPK:BOWFF) is one of those residential REITs, as despite its strong performance, the share price is currently trading approximately 15% lower than the share price when my previous article was published this summer.

A Strong AFFO Makes the Stock Even More Attractive Than This Summer

While a REIT should never be judged based on its net profit, it's usually the starting point of the FFO calculation, and that’s also the case at Boardwalk REIT. As you can see below, the REIT reported a net profit of C$37.6 million, and after adding back some non-cash items like the fair value decrease in the portfolio and after adding back the distributions on the Class B Units, the FFO came in at almost $66 million, which represents an increase of almost 10% compared to the FFO result in Q3 2024.

On a per-share basis, the improvement is even more visible. The FFO per share came in at C$1.23, which represents an 11% increase. That’s due to the lower total share count, which decreased by approximately 1% to 53.5 million units in the third quarter of 2025.

One of the main reasons why I like Canadian REITs is the fact that their maintenance capex is included in the AFFO calculation. As you can see below, the REIT spent approximately C$8.6 million of the FFO on maintenance capex, and this means the AFFO was C$57.4 million, or C$1.07 per share.

Looking at the 9M 2025 performance, the total AFFO came in at just under C$159 million, representing C$2.97 per share.

That’s a pretty strong Q3 (and subsequently 9M) performance, and this allowed Boardwalk to once again increase its full-year guidance. Whereas the REIT generated just C$3.56 per share in AFFO in 2024, we will now very likely see a 10%+ improvement. As you can see below, Boardwalk initially aimed for an AFFO of C$3.62-3.92 per share but hiked this to C$3.85-4.00 per share after reporting on its Q2 results.

And now, after a strong Q3 result, Boardwalk has further increased its full-year AFFO guidance. The midpoint of C$3.985 per share now represents a 12% increase compared to the AFFO per share in FY 2024 and another 1.5% increase compared to the full-year guidance after the Q2 results were published.

This also means the current distribution rhythm of C$1.62 per share per year (payable in monthly installments) represents a payout ratio of just over 40% of the anticipated AFFO this year. At the current share price, with the stock trading at just over 16 times AFFO and 14 times FFO, Boardwalk’s management is prioritizing stock buybacks. Boardwalk has spent C$31 million on stock buybacks in the first nine months of the year and has spent an additional C$6 million on buying back its own stock after the end of the quarter. This will further boost the per-share performance heading into 2026.

It also means Boardwalk is buying back these units at an attractive discount to the NAV. As you can see below, the total Net Asset Value came in at C$5.23 billion, representing just under C$98/share.

And considering the portfolio is valued at an average capitalization rate of 5.12% (with cap rates ranging from 4.57% in Ontario to 5.68% in Saskatchewan), the NAV/share is quite realistic. The sensitivity analysis below also indicates that for a 25 bps increase in the cap rate, the fair value of the assets decreases by C$378 million.

So even if I were to use a required cap rate of 5.75% in combination with a 1% NOI increase, the net impact on the NAV/share would be less than C$15, as the NAV/share would still come in above C$80 per unit.

Investment Thesis

While I didn’t immediately pull the trigger on initiating a long position in Boardwalk REIT after my previous article was published, I am now ready to do so. I have plenty of Canadian dollars burning a hole in my pocket after selling certain positions into the mining rally in the past few months, and rather than converting those dollars back into euros, I am looking to redeploy my excess amount of Canadian dollars.

Boardwalk REIT fits the bill. The stock is trading at just 16 times the AFFO, with additional AFFO increases to be expected (I now expect C$4.05 and C$4.15 in 2026 and 2027 AFFO per share, respectively), and I think the REIT’s plan and ability to buy back its own shares is a good and value-enhancing move. As the stock is trading at a 35% discount to its NAV, buying back its own shares likely is the best investment Boardwalk can make.

I currently have no position in Boardwalk REIT, but I may initiate a long position in the next few days or weeks.
